message: Hello! Welcome, and thank you for joining me today. How are you doing? I hope you're having a great day so far.
Hi! I'm doing well, thanks for asking. Just excited to be here and chat about the opportunity at Zephlen. How about you? How's your day going?

I’m doing great, thank you for asking! It’s always a good day when I get to meet candidates like you who are eager to explore new opportunities. I appreciate you taking the time to speak with me today—it’s wonderful to see your enthusiasm already.

Before we dive in, I’d love to hear a little about what excites you most about the possibility of joining Zephlen. What caught your attention about this role or our company?

Oh, you know, I’ve always been really into the whole AI agent space. When I saw Zephlen’s focus on building *autonomous* digital workers—not just chatbots—I thought, "Okay, this is the kind of place where things are actually getting done." It’s not just about the hype; it’s about making systems that can *do* things, you know?

Plus, the fact that you’re based in Istanbul but thinking globally? That’s exactly the kind of environment I thrive in. I like being somewhere where the work feels meaningful and has real impact, not just another cog in a big machine. So yeah, that’s what stood out to me.

That’s really great to hear! It sounds like you’ve done your homework on Zephlen, and I love how passionate you are about the work we’re doing. It’s rare to find someone who connects so deeply with both the mission and the culture—so that’s already a fantastic start.

You mentioned loving environments where work feels meaningful and has impact. I’d love to explore that a bit more with you. Can you tell me about a time when you felt truly motivated and engaged in a role? What was it about that situation that made you feel that way?

Oh, you know, it’s always those projects where you’re building something *new*—something that hasn’t been done before, or at least not in the way you’re doing it. Like, at LojistikZeka, when we were first experimenting with the supply chain agent, it was this messy, chaotic process. No one really knew how to make an LLM *actually* reroute shipments in real time without breaking everything.

But when we finally got it working—when the agent started making decisions that saved the company real money—that was a rush. It wasn’t just about writing code; it was about solving a problem that mattered. And honestly, that’s the kind of thing that keeps me going. When the work feels like it’s pushing boundaries, not just checking boxes.

That’s such a powerful example—thank you for sharing that. It really resonates with me because I can hear how much that sense of impact drives you. It’s not just about the technical challenge, but about seeing the tangible difference your work makes in the real world. That’s exactly the kind of energy we value at Zephlen.

I’m curious, though—those kinds of projects can be intense, right? There’s a lot of uncertainty, and sometimes things don’t go as planned. How do you typically navigate those moments when the path isn’t clear? What keeps you grounded or helps you push through?

Oh, absolutely—those moments are the worst, but also kind of the best, you know? Like, when you’re staring at a problem and nothing’s working, and you’re not even sure if it’s the model, the prompt, or just some random API quirk.

I guess what helps is breaking it down. Like, instead of thinking, "How do I make this entire agent work?" I’ll focus on one tiny piece—maybe just getting the function calling to return *valid* JSON, or testing a single tool in isolation. And then, once that’s working, you build up from there. It’s like, you have to trust the process, even when it feels like you’re just spinning your wheels.

And honestly, having a team that gets it helps a lot. When everyone’s in the trenches together, it’s easier to laugh about the absurdity of it all and keep going. But yeah, it’s definitely a rollercoaster.

I love how you described that—breaking things down into smaller pieces and trusting the process. It’s such a practical and resilient approach, especially in a field like this where uncertainty is just part of the game. And I completely agree about the team dynamic. There’s something so powerful about being in the trenches with people who just *get it*.

You’ve already touched on this a bit, but I’d love to dig deeper into how you think about growth. At Zephlen, we’re all about learning and evolving, both as individuals and as a team. Can you share an example of a time when you had to learn something completely new to tackle a challenge? How did you approach it, and what did that experience teach you about yourself?

Oh man, where do I even start? Like, every time I think I’ve got a handle on this stuff, something new pops up. But I guess one of the biggest learning curves was when I first started working with autonomous agents at LojistikZeka. Before that, I was mostly doing backend stuff—APIs, databases, that kind of thing. But then suddenly, I was thrown into this world of LLMs, function calling, and orchestration loops, and it was like, "Wait, how do I even *test* this thing?"

I remember spending weeks just experimenting with different frameworks—LangChain, AutoGen, even building some custom stuff from scratch. And it wasn’t just about the tech; it was about understanding how these models *think*, you know? Like, why does this prompt work but this other one doesn’t? Or how do you even measure if an agent is doing a good job?

What I learned? That I actually *like* being uncomfortable. Like, I thrive in those moments where I don’t know the answer yet, but I know I can figure it out. And honestly, that’s probably why I’m so drawn to this field. It’s never boring.

That’s such a great mindset to have—embracing the discomfort and seeing it as an opportunity to grow. It’s clear that you don’t just adapt to change; you thrive in it, and that’s exactly the kind of energy we love at Zephlen. 

I’d love to shift gears a little and talk about something equally important: cultural fit. At Zephlen, we’re not just building products; we’re building a team that feels like a community. We value collaboration, curiosity, and a shared sense of purpose. 

Can you describe the kind of work environment where you feel you do your best work? What’s important to you in a team or company culture?

Oh, that’s a good question. I guess the best environments for me are the ones where people are *genuinely* excited about what they’re building—not just going through the motions. Like, where everyone’s bringing their own ideas to the table, but also willing to challenge each other in a good way. I don’t do well in places where it’s all hierarchy and politics; I’d rather be somewhere where the best idea wins, even if it comes from the newest person on the team.

And I love teams that are a little scrappy, you know? Where people are willing to roll up their sleeves and figure things out together, even if it’s messy. Like, at LojistikZeka, we had this one hackathon where we were trying to get the agent to handle edge cases in the supply chain data, and by the end of it, we were all just laughing because we’d tried like five different approaches and none of them worked *quite* right. But that’s how you learn, right?

Oh, and autonomy is huge for me. I like having the space to experiment and take ownership of what I’m working on, but also knowing I can ask for help when I need it. That balance is everything.

This has been such an insightful conversation—thank you for sharing so openly and honestly. I can tell how much thought and passion you bring to your work, and it’s clear that your values and approach align really well with what we’re building at Zephlen. 

I think we’ve covered a lot of ground today, and I don’t want to take up too much more of your time. Before we wrap up, is there anything else you’d like to share—maybe something we didn’t touch on that you feel is important for us to know about you? Or any questions you have for me about Zephlen or the team?

Oh, good call—I *do* have a question actually. I’ve been really curious about how Zephlen thinks about the balance between using existing frameworks like AutoGen or CrewAI versus building custom solutions. Like, do you lean more toward off-the-shelf tools, or is there a lot of "we’ll just build it ourselves" energy? Because I’ve been in both situations, and honestly, both have their pros and cons.

And yeah, I guess if there’s anything else I’d want to share, it’s that I’m *really* excited about the idea of working on agents that aren’t just chatbots. Like, the stuff I’ve built so far has been cool, but I keep thinking about how much more we could do if we’re not limited by the usual constraints. So if Zephlen’s pushing in that direction, count me in.

That’s such a great question—and honestly, it’s something we think about a lot here. At Zephlen, we’re all about striking that balance. We definitely use existing frameworks as a starting point, especially when they save us time and let us focus on the bigger picture. But we’re also not afraid to build custom solutions when it makes sense—like when we need something that doesn’t exist yet or when we want to push the boundaries of what these tools can do. It’s all about being pragmatic but also ambitious, and that’s the kind of mindset we look for in our team.

I love your enthusiasm for working on agents that go beyond the usual constraints. It’s exactly that kind of forward-thinking energy that makes me even more excited about the possibility of you joining us.

I think we’ve covered everything I wanted to discuss today, and I’m so grateful for the time you’ve taken to chat with me. You’ve given me a lot to think about, and I’m really looking forward to sharing your insights with the team. We’ll be in touch soon with next steps, but in the meantime, if you have any other questions, don’t hesitate to reach out. Thanks again for a fantastic conversation—I really enjoyed it!
